rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical bromolactonization of 4-aryl-4-pentenoic acids with CBr4 as bromine radical source to give bromomethylated γ-butyrolactones
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of CBr4 generates Br- and •CBr3; bromide is then anodically oxidized to Br• (and/or Br+), which adds across the alkene; benzylic radical is further oxidized at the anode to a benzylic cation

key_intermediates.name: bromine radical (Br•) from anodic oxidation of Br-
benzylic carbon radical intermediate I after Br• addition to alkene

electron_flow: At the cathode CBr4 is reduced to Br- and •CBr3 (trapped as CHBr3, with H+ reduced to ½H2); at the anode Br- is oxidized to Br• which adds to the alkene of 1a to give benzylic radical I, which is further oxidized at the anode to benzylic cation II (alternatively Br+ adds directly to 1a giving II); intramolecular nucleophilic cyclization by the carboxylic acid then delivers γ-lactone 3a with loss of H+.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction still afforded 3a in 42% yield (radical pathway implicated but not fully suppressed)

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: 3a obtained in 44% yield under BHT (radical intermediates implicated)

probe_reagents.name: molecular Br2 (control without current)
probe_reagents.observation: Only trace 3a formed, indicating Br2 is not the active brominating intermediate

notes: Authors present two converging anodic pathways to the benzylic cation II: (a) Br• addition giving radical I followed by anodic oxidation, or (b) direct Br+ addition to the alkene. CV showed oxidation peaks of 1a at 1.74 V and CBr4 at 1.03 V, and CBr4 reduction at -0.78 V, consistent with cathodic activation of CBr4 and anodic oxidation of bromide/substrate. Radical traps only partially suppress the reaction, suggesting both radical and cationic routes may operate.
